title: Welcome to Premed

objectives.statement: Describe the recommended Premed subject journey and explain at least two ways one stage prepares the next.
Explain why representative concepts from the Premed subjects matter for later medical study.
Identify the scientific knowledge and concrete capabilities learners should be able to use by course completion.

scenes.title: Welcome to Premed
scenes.contentHtml:
Welcome to Premed

Welcome to Premed. You are beginning a journey through science that makes the human body more understandable and evidence more trustworthy.

You need not know these subjects yet. This welcome shows what you will learn, why each earns its place, and what you can do when they connect. The course begins with careful questions, moves through matter and living systems, and ends with you using evidence on unfamiliar problems.

Here is one glimpse of a finished explanation. If a wider tube carries more liquid in a model, evidence tests the pattern, physics explains how width affects flow, and physiology asks how living tissues and regulation change the picture. Connecting those contributions and limits turns separate facts into an explanation.

The route is easier to hold in three stages: evidence and measurement; matter and living systems; people, populations, and capstone work. The stage shape—not the unfamiliar term inventory—is the target for this welcome. If a term is new, place it in its named subject and note what that subject will later help explain; you will practise that move near the end.

Here is the journey ahead.

  • Scientific method and laboratory practice: You will ask testable questions, choose variables and fair comparisons, measure carefully, keep reproducible records, and decide what a result supports. These habits keep evidence open to inspection and challenge.
  • Mathematics and statistics: You will use units, ratios, graphs, probability, averages, variation, and uncertainty ranges to read research, compare groups, check answers, and separate a numerical pattern from a justified conclusion.
  • Physics: You will use forces and motion; pressure, fluids, and transport; electricity; waves, sound, and light; heat; and radiation to explain movement, blood flow, electrical signals, hearing, vision, measurement, and imaging.
  • General chemistry: You will connect atoms, bonds, molecular shape, reactions, amounts, gases, solutions, energy changes, reaction rates, equilibrium, acids, and bases to the molecular setting of cells and to laboratory, biology, and biochemistry calculations.
  • Organic chemistry: You will trace how molecular shape, recurring groups, electron movement, and three-dimensional arrangement affect carbon-based molecules, and how separation and instrument patterns reveal structure. This prepares reasoning about why molecules of life bind and change.
  • Biochemistry: You will connect proteins and enzymes, membranes, carbohydrates, fats, nucleotides, metabolism, and biological information to explain how cells use energy, regulate reactions, communicate, and respond to molecular change.
  • Biology and genetics: You will follow cell organization, membranes, division, signalling, inheritance, gene expression, variation, and evolution to explain how living systems preserve information, develop different functions, and vary across individuals and populations.
  • Human structure and physiology: You will connect tissues and organs to transport, internal control, nerves, hormones, movement, circulation, breathing, fluid balance, digestion, reproduction, and development. The aim is to explain whole-body mechanisms, not make clinical decisions.
  • Microbiology and immunology: You will compare microbes and viruses, growth, transmission, evolution, barriers, rapid general defences, and slower targeted responses to explain infection and immunity across an agent, host, environment, and time.
  • Behaviour, society, and population health: You will examine perception, learning, stress, relationships, institutions, inequality, access, environment, and population evidence. These help explain why biology alone cannot account for every pattern and why a group association is not one person's experience.
  • Integration and capstone work: You will bring subjects together around an unfamiliar, non-diagnostic problem, inspect sources and data, calculate or model, explain mechanisms, communicate uncertainty, and revise after critique.

The destination is demanding but concrete. By completion, you should be able to explain important scientific relationships, model them with words, quantities, diagrams, or data, investigate questions with honest methods and records, and integrate several subjects when one alone is not enough.

Nobody expects you to begin at the end. Curiosity, careful practice, and a willingness to correct your reasoning are enough to start.

Source Note
claims claim-subject-scope, claim-subject-preparation, claim-completion-capabilitiessources source-premed-syllabus

Adapted under CC BY 4.0 from the Premed syllabus's subject breadth, preparation, and course destination.

scenes.title: How the subjects prepare you for what follows
scenes.contentHtml:
How the subjects prepare you for what follows

The recommended journey has a broad order, although some subjects run together. Each stage supplies ideas for later explanations.

  1. Begin with scientific inquiry, laboratory practice, mathematics, and statistics. You learn to frame a question, compare fairly, measure with units, read graphs, describe variation, and preserve an honest record. The same habits let you test a chemical prediction, interpret a genetics result, or challenge a population claim.
  2. Study general chemistry and introductory biology side by side. Chemistry explains particles, bonds, reactions, solutions, and energy. Biology introduces molecules, membranes, cells, transport, and organization. Together they connect a biological outcome with interactions that could produce it.
  3. Add physics while chemistry and biology deepen. Forces, energy, pressure, flow, electricity, waves, and light make measurements and body processes easier to model. Equilibrium, acids and bases, inheritance, and gene expression add regulation and continuity. These subjects help you test whether an explanation is chemically, physically, and biologically possible.
  4. Use organic chemistry to enter biochemistry. Carbon-based structures, functional groups, three-dimensional shape, and reaction patterns prepare you to reason about proteins, membranes, nucleic acids, and metabolism. Biochemistry then shows how those molecules cooperate in a regulated cell and how evidence can test the explanation.
  5. Carry those foundations into human structure, physiology, microbiology, and immunology. Tissues and organs depend on transport, energy, signalling, and control. Infection and defence depend on cells, molecules, evolution, and population change. Earlier ideas now help explain whole-body function.
  6. Broaden the account with behaviour, society, and population health. People live in social and physical environments, so later medical study needs evidence about behaviour, institutions, access, inequality, and populations. This helps you distinguish a molecular mechanism, an individual experience, and a population pattern.
  7. Finish with integration and capstone work. A new problem will not announce which subject to use. You must choose relevant ideas, connect them through cause or evidence, inspect limitations, and communicate a conclusion another person can audit.

The pace can vary, and later lessons will revisit earlier ideas.

Source Note
claims claim-recommended-sequence, claim-subject-preparationsources source-premed-syllabus

Adapted under CC BY 4.0 from the syllabus's recommended sequence and prerequisites; institutions may order subjects differently.

scenes.title: What learning will look like
scenes.contentHtml:
What learning will look like

Before you preview how the course will work, retrieve one reason for its order.

Check
id check-sequence-why

Pause here. Without looking back, cover the following text and commit a one-sentence answer before continuing: why does organic chemistry come before much of biochemistry, and why do inquiry and quantitative reasoning begin before both?

Once you have answered, compare: organic chemistry prepares you to reason about carbon-based structures and reactions, while inquiry and quantitative reasoning provide methods for testing, measuring, and interpreting claims. If you repeated only the order, add why each step helps.

You will learn by meeting an explanation, retrieving it without looking, solving or modelling something, inspecting evidence, receiving feedback, and trying again with less support.

Practical work may be a physical investigation, simulation, or supplied dataset or observation. All can build question-design and analysis skills, but only physical work shows a hands-on action. Your record will name the kind completed; this welcome requires no physical work.

Here is a preview using a fictional training dataset. It requires no prior science. This previews the course's reasoning style; the welcome check does not score it.

Worked Example
id worked-example-flow-previewlabel A model-flow question from start to finish

Task. A simulation description says it uses the same liquid, pump setting, tube length, and ten-second collection time for two tubes. Tube A is narrow and Tube B is wider.

Run Tube A Tube B
1 20 mL 79 mL
2 19 mL 82 mL
3 21 mL 78 mL

The question is: Does tube width affect the volume collected in this model?

Represent what is given. Width is the planned difference. The description claims that liquid, tube length, pump setting, and collection time stay constant; the table reports volume. The pattern cannot verify those conditions.

Choose a principle and plan. A fair comparison changes the tested feature while keeping the other conditions the same. Compare all three runs, then keep the conclusion inside the simulation.

Work through the evidence. Tube A stays near 20 millilitres and Tube B near 80. Every Tube B result is about four times its paired Tube A result. The difference between tubes is much larger than the small run-to-run variation.

Check the reasoning. The units match and no run reverses the pattern. An investigator would inspect settings or logs to verify the claimed pump, liquid, tube length, and time; the data pattern cannot. If a log showed another difference, width would not be the only planned explanation.

Interpret and limit. The wider tube allowed more liquid to pass in this model under these settings. The data do not establish an exact law or tell us what happens in a human blood vessel. Later physics, chemistry, and physiology add pressure, resistance, fluids, tissues, and biological regulation.

Self-explanation. Pause here. Cover the following paragraph and commit an answer before continuing: which choice protects the conclusion most—collecting a large number, using millilitres, or holding the other conditions steady—and why?

Once you have answered, compare: holding the other conditions steady protects the conclusion because that makes width the planned difference. Units make the measurement interpretable and repetition reveals variation, but neither fixes a comparison in which the pump or liquid also changed.

Check
id check-faded-comparison

Pause here. Cover the following paragraph and commit an answer before continuing. A second dataset compares a narrow tube at a low pump setting with a wide tube at a high setting. Can it isolate the effect of width? Answer yes or no, name the competing difference, and propose a repair.

Once you have answered, compare: No. Pump setting changes with width, so either could explain the result. Repeat the comparison at one pump setting. If you named only the problem, add the repaired comparison.

This is the course's reasoning pattern in miniature: define the question, choose relevant ideas, work carefully, check, interpret, and state limits.

Source Note
claims claim-recommended-sequence, claim-subject-preparation, claim-learning-practice, claim-completion-capabilitiessources source-premed-syllabus

Adapted under CC BY 4.0 from the syllabus's recommended sequence, subject preparation, practical modalities, and learning practices; the table is an original fictional example.

scenes.title: The destination and your first check
scenes.contentHtml:
The destination and your first check

By the end of Premed, knowing the subjects means being able to use them. You should be able to:

  • Explain relationships from molecules to populations with cause-and-evidence language.
  • Model a relationship with words, quantities, graphs, diagrams, chemical structures, or data, and state what the model leaves out.
  • Investigate a testable question with suitable measurements, comparisons, auditable records, and conclusions no stronger than the evidence.
  • Integrate subjects in an unfamiliar problem, distinguish mechanism from association, communicate clearly, and revise after critique.

Premed prepares you for further study. It does not authorize diagnosis or treatment, award university credit or a professional licence, or guarantee admission.

Check
id check-welcome-transfer

Pause here. Read the task, then cover the comparison below and commit your answer before continuing.

A friend asks, “Why isn't Premed just biology?” Without looking back, give a brief answer that:

  1. names the subjects that begin, develop, and complete the journey;
  2. gives one example of a concept preparing later medical study;
  3. names all four completion capabilities and two actions a finisher should perform; and
  4. states one thing Premed completion does not authorize, award, or guarantee.

You do not need to explain any science you have not learned yet.

Compare: begin with inquiry and quantitative work; develop chemical, physical, biological, human, microbial, behavioural, social, and population subjects; and end with capstone work. Include one preparation link, such as pressure and flow preparing circulation. Name all four capabilities—explain, model, investigate, and integrate—and two finisher actions, such as explaining a relationship and investigating a question with a fair comparison. Add one completion boundary, such as “Premed does not authorize diagnosis or treatment.” If you only listed subjects, add why a transition matters; if you omitted a capability, an action, or the boundary, add it before moving on.

Misconception
id misconception-prior-science-gate

Commit first. Before reading the worry, answer in one line: must a beginner already explain unfamiliar scientific terms to be ready for this welcome? Write yes or no and one reason.

Worry: “If I cannot already explain enzymes, electricity, inheritance, or immunity, I am not ready.”

What the worry cannot explain: a beginner can complete today's actual task by placing an unfamiliar term in the course journey and saying what its subject will later help explain. That does not require knowing the term's mechanism in advance.

Repair: this welcome asks you to describe the journey, not perform untaught science. Place an unfamiliar term in its subject and say what the subject will unlock. For enzyme regulation, “Biochemistry will teach how cells control reaction helpers called enzymes” is enough. You need not explain the actual enzyme mechanism today; naming the teaching subject and what it will later help explain is sufficient.

Recheck. Choose a different unfamiliar term. Pause, cover the following text, and commit one line that names the subject that will teach it and what it will help explain.

Compare: name a different term, its teaching subject, and a later explanation—not the science itself.

The welcome check

The welcome check has three untimed responses worth ten points. To complete it, score at least 8 out of 10 and earn at least one point on each response. You may retake it; it checks your orientation, not your prior science.

The scoring requirements are visible before you begin:

  • For the journey response, name both inquiry or laboratory work and quantitative work in the opening; at least three middle subject areas, including at least one chemical or physical area and at least one biological area; at least two later medicine-facing domains; integration or capstone work as the final stage; and at least two accurate because/how preparation links.
  • For the relevance response, choose four concepts from four different subject areas and give a specific concept-to-purpose link for all four.
  • For the capabilities response, name all four capabilities, give one observable action for each, and state one accurate completion boundary.

On a retry, use different prompts:

  • advise an incoming learner how to travel from trustworthy evidence to the capstone, naming representative subject areas at each stage;
  • choose different subjects or concepts and explain what each unlocks;
  • apply the four capabilities to a new non-diagnostic capstone scenario.

Accessibility and alternatives

Choose paragraphs, bullets, or an outline. Type, paste from assistive technology, or use device dictation; audio upload is unavailable. No task requires personal health information, speed, sight alone, or physical work. Headings and lists preserve reading order for keyboard, screen-reader, reflow, zoom, narrow-screen, and print use; words explain the table.

Recovery route

If names blur, use three stages: evidence and measurement; matter and living systems; people, populations, and capstone work. Return to the complete subject list in Welcome to Premed and complete: “I will learn ___ in ___ because it helps me later explain or evaluate ___.” Then try again from memory.

If the completion capabilities blur, use the four verbs as prompts: explain a relationship and why it holds; model a relationship and name what the model leaves out; investigate with a fair comparison, an auditable record, and a bounded conclusion; integrate several subjects in an unfamiliar problem. Write one action for each verb, then cover the list and reconstruct all four.

If the completion boundary blurs, reread the boundary sentence above and complete: “Premed prepares me for ___, but course completion does not ___.” Check that your ending does not claim diagnosis, treatment, credit, admission, a professional licence, or professional authority, then try a different ending from memory.

Source Note
claims claim-subject-scope, claim-learning-practice, claim-completion-capabilities, claim-orientation-boundarysources source-premed-syllabus

items.prompt: Without looking back, describe the recommended Premed subject journey to a new learner in your own words. In the opening, identify both inquiry or laboratory work and quantitative work. Name at least three middle foundation areas, including at least one chemical or physical area (general chemistry, physics, or organic chemistry) and at least one biological area (biochemistry or biology/genetics). Name at least two later medicine-facing domains (human structure/physiology, microbiology/immunology, or behaviour/society/population health), and identify integration or capstone work as the final stage. Give at least two explicit because/how links showing how one named stage prepares another; order alone is not enough.

answer.correct: A complete response identifies inquiry or laboratory work and quantitative work at the opening; correctly places and names at least three middle groups from general chemistry, physics, organic chemistry, biochemistry, and biology/genetics, including at least one chemical or physical group and at least one biological group; names at least two later domains from human structure/physiology, microbiology/immunology, and behaviour/society/population health; names integration/capstone as the final stage; and explains at least two accurate preparation links.
answer.summary: The journey moves from trustworthy evidence and measurement through matter and living systems to people, populations, and an unfamiliar capstone problem.
answer.reasoning: Inquiry or laboratory work and quantitative work come first because later subjects use measurements and evidence. The middle needs enough correctly placed chemical, physical, and biological breadth to show how matter and living systems are developed, without requiring every label. The later stage needs representative medicine-facing domains before integration and capstone work bring the foundations together. Two explicit because/how links show preparation rather than chronology alone. On retry, advise an incoming learner how to proceed from trustworthy evidence to a capstone, still naming representative subject areas at each stage without reproducing the original response.
acceptableResponses: Prose, bullets, or an outline using ordinary-language equivalents that meets the stated opening, representative middle, representative later, final-stage, and two-link requirements while preserving the broad recommended chronology.
commonErrors: Giving an unordered subject list with no explanation of why the order helps
Naming too few representative middle or later subject groups, or omitting integration and capstone work as the final stage
Beginning with advanced medical content instead of inquiry, laboratory, and quantitative work

items.prompt: Choose four representative concepts from four different Premed subject areas. In plain language, explain what each concept helps unlock for later medical study; all four concept-to-purpose links are required. You are describing relevance, not teaching the detailed science.

answer.correct: The response identifies four representative concepts from four different subject areas and gives a defensible later-study link for each, such as uncertainty to research interpretation, pressure and flow to circulation, molecular shape to biomolecule behaviour, gene expression to cell differences, or social conditions to population health patterns.
answer.summary: Representative concepts earn their place by enabling later explanations, measurements, or evidence judgments.
answer.reasoning: This task asks for a concept-to-purpose relationship. Naming a subject is not enough; each example must say what the concept later helps explain or evaluate. A retry should use different subjects or concepts so that the learner reconstructs the relationship instead of copying feedback.
acceptableResponses: Any four accurate concept-and-relevance pairs drawn from four different named subject areas in the lesson.
commonErrors: Listing concepts without saying what they unlock
Making clinical claims instead of describing preparation for study
Using four concepts from only one subject

items.prompt: A learner says that completing Premed means remembering a large set of facts. Correct that account. State the four main completion capabilities, give one concrete action that shows each capability, and state one thing course completion does not authorize, award, or guarantee.

answer.correct: Completion requires explaining scientific relationships, modelling them with appropriate representations, investigating questions with auditable methods, and integrating several subjects in unfamiliar problems. Each capability must be tied to an observable action, and the response must state one accurate boundary: completion does not authorize diagnosis or treatment, award university credit or a professional licence, guarantee admission, confer clinical competence, or grant professional authority.
answer.summary: Premed aims at explanation, modelling, investigation, and integration rather than recall alone.
answer.reasoning: Facts support the four capabilities but do not replace them. Concrete evidence includes explaining a relationship, using and limiting a graph or model, designing and documenting a fair investigation, and connecting several domains in a new problem. The explicit completion boundary prevents a study-preparation course from being mistaken for a credential or professional authorization. On retry, apply the four capabilities to a new non-diagnostic capstone scenario, give one action for each, and include one accurate completion boundary rather than repeating the original response.
acceptableResponses: Equivalent wording that identifies explain, model, investigate, and integrate, gives an observable example of each, and states one accurate limit on what completion authorizes, awards, or guarantees.
commonErrors: Treating course completion as recall or time spent
Naming the four verbs without concrete actions
Claiming that completion grants clinical competence, credit, admission, a professional licence, or professional authority

criteria.description: Award 1 point only when the response identifies both inquiry or laboratory work and quantitative work in the opening stage, accepting ordinary-language equivalents; award 0 if either kind of work is missing or the opening is described only as unspecified basics.

Award 1 point only when the response correctly places and names at least three of these five middle subject groups—general chemistry, physics, organic chemistry, biochemistry, and biology/genetics. For this rule, at least one named group must be general chemistry, physics, or organic chemistry, and at least one must be biochemistry or biology/genetics. Award 0 if fewer than three groups are named, either required breadth category is absent, or the groups are placed outside the middle stage.

Award 1 point only when the response names at least two of these three later domains—human structure/physiology, microbiology/immunology, and behaviour/society/population health—and also names integration/capstone as the final stage; award 0 if fewer than two later domains are named, they are not placed in the later stage, or integration/capstone is not named as final.

Award 1 point only when at least two named stage pairs are joined by explicit, accurate because/how relationships explaining preparation; award 0 for fewer than two such links, or for restating order without explaining preparation.

Award 1 point only when four distinct concepts are each paired with a different named Premed subject area; award 0 if there are fewer than four concepts, fewer than four subject areas, or subject names without concepts.

Award 1 point only when every one of the four concepts has an accurate, specific explanation of how it supports later medical study; award 0 if any concept lacks a purpose or is linked only by a vague claim that it matters.

Award 1 point only when all four links are understandable in plain language, remain at orientation depth, and make no unsupported clinical claim; award 0 if jargon hides a link, detailed untaught science is asserted, or any clinical claim exceeds preparation for study.

Award 1 point only when the response identifies all four capabilities—explaining, modelling, investigating, and integrating, or clear equivalents; award 0 if any capability is missing or replaced by recall alone.

Award 1 point only when each of the four capabilities has at least one observable scientific action that demonstrates it; award 0 if any capability has no action or is illustrated only by reading, remembering, or spending time.

Award 1 point only when the response explicitly states one accurate thing course completion does not authorize, award, or guarantee, consistent with the lesson's credit, admission, clinical-competence, diagnosis/treatment, professional-licence, or professional-authority boundaries. Award 0 when this requested boundary is absent because the evidence is missing. Also award 0 when the response contradicts the boundary by claiming any such entitlement; flag that contradiction for correction rather than treating it as a mere omission.

sources.title: Premed: Foundations for Medical Study

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: The lesson intentionally retains and adapts the syllabus's named subject sequence and its explain, model, investigate, and integrate destination. Full attribution, a CC BY 4.0 link, and a modification notice are provided in ATTRIBUTION.md; the lesson adds original teaching prose, examples, prompts, recovery guidance, and assessment design.

agentAccess.notes: The source was read from the local authorized repository checkout. Its repository content licence expressly permits sharing and adaptation with attribution and change notice, and no source-specific term prohibiting this agent access was found on the exact local route used.

claims.statement: The Premed syllabus includes scientific method and laboratory practice; mathematics and statistics; physics; general chemistry; organic chemistry; biochemistry; biology and genetics; human structure and physiology; microbiology and immunology; behaviour, society, and population health; and integration and capstone work.
claims.scope: EmbeddedKnowledge Premed curriculum version 1.1 candidate, including its common core, medicine-facing bridge, route-specific behavioural material or supplied primer, and integration work.
claims.uncertainty: Behavioural and social depth varies by selected route; the welcome names the subject area without presenting every route-specific module as portable common core.

claims.statement: Within the recommended Premed journey, inquiry and quantitative reasoning support later evidence use; chemical, physical, biological, organic, and biochemical foundations prepare later work with human systems, infection, immunity, behaviour, populations, and unfamiliar capstone problems.
claims.scope: Instructional dependencies and medicine-facing uses declared in the EmbeddedKnowledge syllabus, not universal admissions prerequisites.
claims.uncertainty: Some modules may run concurrently, and institution-specific prerequisite sequences can differ.

claims.statement: The recommended sequence begins with the welcome and scientific inquiry, develops chemistry, biology, and physics, then organic chemistry and biochemistry, continues through a medicine-facing bridge and a selected route, and ends with integration and capstone work.
claims.scope: Recommended module order in EmbeddedKnowledge Premed curriculum version 1.1 candidate.
claims.uncertainty: The syllabus permits concurrent modules and pace variation after shared prerequisites.

claims.statement: Premed uses explanation, retrieval, modelling, problem solving, physical or simulated investigations, supplied datasets or observations, feedback, retry, and accessibility alternatives; only physical work demonstrates hands-on actions, so practical modality is reported.
claims.scope: Course learning design, practical curriculum, accessibility provisions, and recovery rules in the syllabus.
claims.uncertainty: The exact available physical activities and accommodations depend on delivery context and approved safety conditions.

claims.statement: The Premed destination is to explain, model, investigate, and integrate foundational science, using evidence and communicating limits in unfamiliar but bounded problems.
claims.scope: The course promise, program-level outcomes, WEL-000 purpose, and capstone destination in the syllabus.
claims.uncertainty: This is the intended curricular destination; demonstrated completion still depends on later course evidence and does not follow from finishing the welcome lesson.

claims.statement: The welcome is a motivating orientation rather than a prior-science gate, and Premed completion does not itself grant university credit, admission, clinical competence, or professional authority.
claims.scope: WEL-000 completion evidence and syllabus completion interpretation.

terms.definition: An evidence-building process that frames testable questions, compares explanations with observations, and revises conclusions when the evidence requires it; it is not a single rigid recipe.
Methods for describing variation in data and reasoning carefully from observations to bounded conclusions.
A purposeful representation of selected features and relationships used to explain, predict, or test an idea within stated limits.
The study of biological inheritance, variation, genes, and how genetic information contributes to observable traits.
The study of the molecules and chemical processes of living systems, including enzymes, membranes, energy use, and biological information.
The study of how living structures function and regulate their internal conditions across cells, tissues, organs, and systems.
The study of microscopic organisms and related biological entities, including how they grow, change, and interact with hosts and environments.
The study of biological defence, including barriers, rapid responses, targeted responses, tolerance, and immune memory.
The study of health patterns and their distribution across groups, together with the biological, behavioural, social, and environmental conditions related to those patterns.
A culminating task in which a learner selects and connects knowledge from several subjects to address an unfamiliar, bounded problem and defend the reasoning.

attributionHtml:
Attribution

Lesson authorship and licence

Welcome to Premed (PREM-WEL-001), version 0.4.0, was prepared for EmbeddedKnowledge under the accountable principal VSBDev (github:VSBDev). Except where stated below, the lesson prose, fictional training data, prompts, assessment, glossary, and structured records are offered under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International licence.

No third-party image, audio, video, software, or dataset is included in this pack.

Adapted curriculum source

This lesson adapts EmbeddedKnowledge, Premed: Foundations for Medical Study, curriculum version 1.1 candidate (20 July 2026) (source-premed-syllabus), preserved at immutable commit afc8b980e1181d172b0954b7b46bfcdd32d0db85 in the EmbeddedKnowledge repository and licensed there under CC BY 4.0.

Material retained and adapted: the syllabus's named subject sequence, the relationship among successive stages of study, its physical/simulation/dataset practical modalities, and its four-part destination of explaining, modelling, investigating, and integrating science.

Modifications: the syllabus material was reorganized and rewritten as an original 60-minute, four-scene welcome for a novice learner. The adaptation separates the subjects into an explicit plain-language overview, explains how each subject prepares later medical study, supplies a fictional worked dataset, adds retrieval and transfer prompts, creates an original low-stakes assessment and retry forms, and adds learner-visible recovery and accessibility guidance. No syllabus question, table, example, media, or dataset was copied.

Assessment format note: assessment schema v1 has no mastery-threshold or alternate-form field. This draft therefore preserves the 8/10 threshold, the requirement to earn at least one point on every response, and equivalent retry forms in learner scene prose.

Agent assistance and provenance

Codex materially authored the draft under the accountability of VSBDev with this disclosed provenance:

  • System: Codex
  • Provider: OpenAI
  • Model: GPT-5.6-Sol
  • Version: Codex CLI 0.144.6
